file_name: Hartanto and Herawati - 2024 - Comparative analysis of efficientnet and resnet models in the classification of skin cancer.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used pre-trained deep learning models, specifically EfficientNet (B0-B7) and ResNet-50, to classify skin cancer types using the HAM10000 dataset. The study involved training these convolutional neural networks (CNNs) on dermoscopic images to achieve classification accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental results comparing EfficientNet and ResNet-50 models on the HAM10000 dataset. It includes a Research Methodology section detailing data preprocessing, model implementation, and specific accuracy metrics (e.g., EfficientNet B7 achieving 88.41% test accuracy), which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: COMPARATIVE ANALYSIS OF EFFICIENTNET AND RESNET MODELS IN THE CLASSIFICATION OF SKIN CANCER
brief_summary: This study compares the performance of pre-trained EfficientNet (B0-B7) and ResNet-50 models for classifying skin cancer types using the HAM10000 dataset. The researchers preprocessed the dermoscopic images by resizing them to 224x224 pixels and removing hair noise before splitting the data into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing sets. Various additional layers, including Flatten, GlobalAveragePooling2D, and Dropout, were tested to optimize model accuracy. The experimental results demonstrated that EfficientNet models generally outperformed ResNet-50 across different configurations. Specifically, EfficientNet B7 achieved the highest test accuracy of 88.41%, while ResNet-50 achieved the lowest test accuracy of 83.42%. The authors concluded that EfficientNet is more effective than ResNet-50 for skin cancer classification tasks.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer types (7 classes: melanoma, melanocytic nevi, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratoses, benign keratosis-like lesions, dermatofibroma, vascular lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. The models (EfficientNet B0-B7 and ResNet-50) predict discrete diagnostic categories corresponding to the 7 lesion types present in the dataset. Performance is measured using accuracy metrics on training, validation, and test sets.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Skin Cancer: HAM10000)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic images of pigmented skin lesions, obtained from Kaggle. It contains 10,015 images and a CSV file with image names and ground truth classes. The dataset is noted for being unbalanced across classes.
classes_used: Actinic Keratoses and Intraepithelial Carcinoma / Bowen's Disease (AKIEC)

use.training: Used for training EfficientNet (B0-B7) and ResNet-50 models after splitting 80% of the data for training.
use.validation: Used for validation after splitting 10% of the data for validation.
use.testing: Used for testing after splitting 10% of the data for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The comparison between EfficientNet and ResNet-50 models was based solely on descriptive accuracy metrics (train, validation, and test accuracy) without statistical inference.

results_obtained_in_each_dataset.extraction_error: Could not parse a balanced JSON object

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained models used due to insufficient data for some classes in the HAM10000 dataset (e.g., dermatofibroma with only 115 images).

architecture_details.resnet_details: ResNet-50 contains one MaxPool layer, one average pool layer, and forty-eight convolutional layers. Uses skip connections with residual blocks.
architecture_details.scaling_method: EfficientNet employs compound scaling to uniformly scale depth, width, and resolution dimensions.

training_details.batch_size: 32 (default), reduced to 16 for EfficientNet B7 due to training issues

classification_or_output_head.description: Additional layers added to match the number of classes (7 classes: MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images from original 600x450 pixels to 224x224 pixels to match model input requirements
Hair removal (noise removal) from dermoscopic images
Data splitting into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing sets

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The paper mentions 'augmenting the image' and 'augmenting the data' in the Research Methodology and Literature Study sections as part of the preprocessing steps. However, it does not specify the exact geometric transforms, color transforms, or sampling methods used, nor does it provide probabilities or ranges for these techniques.

conclusions: EfficientNet models generally outperformed ResNet-50 in classifying skin cancer types on the HAM10000 dataset.
EfficientNet B7 achieved the highest test accuracy of 88.41% among all tested models and configurations.
ResNet-50 achieved the lowest test accuracy of 83.42% among all tested models and configurations.
The study concludes that EfficientNet is more effective and efficient than ResNet-50 for skin cancer classification tasks.
Training accuracy was consistently higher than validation and test accuracy across all models, indicating potential overfitting or the need for further regularization.
The HAM10000 dataset is unbalanced, which may impact model performance, particularly for classes with fewer samples such as dermatofibroma (115 images).
important_remarks: The HAM10000 dataset is highly imbalanced (e.g., 6705 images for Melanocytic Nevus vs. 115 for Dermatofibroma), which likely biases model performance and limits generalizability for minority classes.
The study relies solely on accuracy metrics; it does not report precision, recall, F1-score, or AUC, which are critical for evaluating performance on imbalanced medical classification tasks.
No statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or confidence intervals) was performed to validate the significance of the performance differences between EfficientNet and ResNet-50.
The paper does not specify the exact data augmentation techniques used, only mentioning 'augmenting the data' generally, which hinders reproducibility.
The authors acknowledge that training accuracy was consistently much higher than validation/test accuracy across all models, indicating potential overfitting.
The study lacks external validation; results are based entirely on a single internal split (80/10/10) of the HAM10000 dataset, limiting claims about clinical robustness.

The conclusion that ResNet-50 is 'not very good' at classifying skin cancer is based on a lower test accuracy (83.42%) compared to EfficientNet B7 (88.41%), but the absolute performance levels are modest for a clinical diagnostic tool.
The paper does not address potential data leakage risks or ensure that patient-level separation was maintained during the train/validation/test split.
